authoringProvenance.author: OpenAI GPT-5.6 Pro — unreviewed candidate generation

items.prompt: A monitoring system must report the maximum total load over every contiguous block of exactly 7 days. Which property is the decisive sliding-window signal?
constraints: The block must contain exactly 7 adjacent days.
Only the maximum total is required.

options.text: Successive candidates overlap and differ by one outgoing value and one incoming value.
The prompt asks for a maximum.
The input values are numeric.
Any solution can name a left and a right index.

feedback.reason: The decisive signal is a fixed-length contiguous candidate whose state can be updated by removing one value and adding one value.
feedback.details: A window is justified by structure, not by the word ‘maximum’. Every legal candidate is a length-7 interval, and moving from days [l..r] to [l+1..r+1] preserves six of seven values. A running sum therefore changes by subtracting the outgoing load and adding the incoming load. Numeric input alone and the existence of two indices do not establish a window invariant. This reasoning transfers to any fixed-length aggregate, including averages, counts, and frequency summaries; it does not require values to be positive because no monotonic shrink rule is used.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.maximum_word: Many non-window problems ask for a maximum. The missing requirement is overlapping contiguous candidates with incrementally maintainable state.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.numeric_input: Numeric values permit arithmetic but do not determine the traversal. Non-contiguous, prefix-based, or dynamic-programming problems can also be numeric.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.two_indices: Two indices are an implementation detail. They become a sliding window only when they delimit a maintained contiguous candidate with a legal movement rule.

items.prompt: Choose any set of positions, not necessarily adjacent, so that no two chosen positions are consecutive and the chosen values have maximum total. Which classification is correct?
constraints: Chosen positions may be separated by arbitrary gaps.
No two chosen positions may be adjacent.

options.text: This is not a sliding-window objective because a candidate is not one contiguous interval; state must compare take/skip choices.
Use a fixed window of length 2 because adjacency is mentioned.
Use a variable window and shrink whenever two chosen positions become adjacent.
Use a frequency-constrained window keyed by position parity.

feedback.reason: A sliding window represents one contiguous range; this objective selects a non-contiguous subset and requires take/skip state.
feedback.details: The output contract is the decisive boundary. A candidate solution may include positions 0, 3, and 7 while excluding the values between them, so no pair of moving boundaries encloses exactly the selected state. A fixed or variable window would force every position between left and right into the candidate. The appropriate reasoning is dynamic: at each position compare the best total that skips it with the best earlier compatible total plus its value. This near miss prevents the shallow rule ‘array plus adjacency language means window’.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.fixed_window_two: A length-2 interval only inspects neighbouring pairs; it cannot represent a globally optimal set containing several separated positions.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.variable_window: Shrinking a contiguous range cannot encode arbitrary take/skip choices inside that range.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.frequency_window: Position parity does not express the no-adjacent selection constraint, and a frequency map still describes all elements inside a contiguous range.

items.prompt: Task A asks for the maximum average of exactly k consecutive readings. Task B asks for the longest consecutive segment containing at most k distinct labels. Which classification is correct?
constraints: Both tasks require a contiguous segment.
Task A fixes length; Task B fixes a validity condition.

options.text: Task A uses a fixed-size window; Task B uses a variable-size frequency-constrained window.
Both use fixed-size windows because each statement contains k.
Both use variable-size windows because both optimize an answer.
Task A uses a variable-size window; Task B uses a fixed-size window.

feedback.reason: In Task A, k is the required length; in Task B, k bounds a maintained property while length is the answer.
feedback.details: The same symbol can play different roles. ‘Exactly k consecutive readings’ fixes the distance between the boundaries, so every legal candidate has the same size. ‘At most k distinct labels’ allows many lengths; expansion adds a label and the left boundary moves only when the distinct-count invariant is violated. The decision is based on whether k constrains size or validity, not on the presence of k itself. This distinction transfers to budgets, duplicate limits, and other at-most constraints.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.both_fixed: Task B can have any length from zero upward; k limits distinct labels, not the number of elements.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.both_variable: Task A has no validity repair loop. Its legal size is predetermined and does not vary with the data.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.a_variable_b_fixed: This reverses the roles of k: Task A fixes size, whereas Task B fixes a property of a variable-length range.

items.prompt: A log analyzer needs the longest contiguous run containing at most three distinct event types. Which signal best identifies the intended pattern?
constraints: Length is maximized subject to an at-most-three-distinct validity rule.

options.text: Maintain event frequencies, expand right, and move left only while the distinct-count limit is violated.
Sort all events so equal types become adjacent.
Use a fixed window of length three.
Use one numeric prefix sum over event identifiers.

feedback.reason: Contiguity, a local frequency constraint, and a longest-valid objective point to a variable frequency-constrained window.
feedback.details: The maintained object is the current contiguous run. Adding the next event changes one frequency; if the number of distinct keys exceeds three, advancing left can restore validity while preserving every possible longer ending at the current right boundary. Sorting would destroy original contiguity, a length-three window confuses the limit with size, and a scalar prefix total cannot recover distinct-count state. The classification transfers to no-repeat, at-most-k-frequency, and bounded-category windows.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_events: Sorting changes which events were adjacent in the original log, so it changes the candidate ranges.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.fixed_three: Three limits distinct types, not run length; a valid run may be much longer than three events.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_total: Distinctness is not derivable from one additive total of identifiers; frequencies or equivalent last-position state are required.

items.prompt: All values are strictly positive. The goal is the shortest contiguous range whose sum is at least T. Why is a variable sliding window a legal candidate?
constraints: Every value is greater than zero.
The objective is minimum length with sum at least T.

options.text: Expanding cannot decrease the sum, and removing the leftmost positive value cannot increase it, so validity changes monotonically with each boundary move.
Any shortest-range problem uses a sliding window.
Positive values make the array effectively sorted.
Additivity alone is sufficient, even if values may be negative.

feedback.reason: Strict positivity gives the monotone sum behaviour needed to expand until valid and then shrink while valid.
feedback.details: For a fixed left boundary, moving right forward only increases the sum. Once the sum reaches T, moving left forward only decreases it, so each removal tests a strictly shorter candidate and eventually makes the window invalid. That monotonicity justifies never moving either pointer backward. The word ‘shortest’ is not sufficient, and additivity without sign restrictions does not guarantee the same boundary behaviour. Nonnegative values, including zero, preserve the non-decreasing/non-increasing property, although zeros may produce equal sums across moves.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.shortest_keyword: Shortest paths, edit problems, and many other minimum objectives are not sliding-window problems; the monotone repair rule is the decisive property.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.positive_means_sorted: Positive values say nothing about value order. They control how the sum changes when boundaries move.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sum_is_additive_only: With negative values, expansion can reduce the sum and removing the leftmost value can increase it, invalidating the simple shrink rule.

items.prompt: An array may contain positive and negative integers. Count all contiguous ranges whose sum is exactly T. Which strategy signal is decisive?
constraints: Every qualifying range must be counted, not only a longest or shortest one.

options.text: Use prefix sums with a frequency map: a range ending at r has sum T when an earlier prefix equals currentPrefix − T.
Expand while the sum is below T and shrink while it is above T.
Use a fixed window of length T.
Sort the values and then apply a window.

feedback.reason: Exact-sum counting with mixed signs lacks a monotone repair rule; prefix-difference frequency preserves all possible starting boundaries.
feedback.details: Contiguity alone does not make a problem a sliding window. With signed values, moving right may raise or lower the sum, and removing the leftmost value may also raise or lower it. A single left boundary cannot safely discard all earlier starts. Prefix sums convert each range sum into prefix[r+1] − prefix[l], and a frequency map counts every prior prefix equal to currentPrefix − T. A simple window becomes appropriate only when extra constraints create a monotone validity boundary, such as nonnegative values for an at-most or at-least threshold objective.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.simple_variable_window: The comparison with T does not tell which boundary move is safe when signs are mixed, so shrinking can discard a start that becomes valid later.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.fixed_window_t: T is a target sum, not a range length; qualifying ranges can have different sizes.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_then_window: Sorting destroys original contiguity and therefore changes which ranges are legal.
